elements.sent-2411-001: We present a minimal composite dark matter model, based on a $SU(N_d)$ dark sector with $n_f$ dark quarks and a heavy t-channel mediator.
elements.sent-2411-002: For $n_f\geq 4$, the dark flavor symmetry guarantees the stability of a subset of the dark pions, which serve as our dark matter candidates.
elements.sent-2411-003: Their relic abundance is determined by co-scattering or co-annihilation with the remaining dark pions, which are unstable and decay.
elements.sent-2411-004: Due to their degenerate masses, the annihilation cross section is suppressed at low temperatures, thereby avoiding stringent constraints from indirect detection and opening up the GeV mass window.
elements.sent-2411-005: The decaying dark pions are naturally long lived.
elements.sent-2411-006: We obtain limits on the model from semi-visible or emerging jet searches and estimate the reach of future probes.
elements.sent-2411-007: Dark matter (DM) remains one of the most significant open problems in modern particle physics.
elements.sent-2411-008: While it provides the most compelling explanation for a wide range of very precise astrophysical and cosmological observations, from the rotation curves of galaxies to the fluctuations in the cosmic microwave background, its microscopic nature remains largely unknown.
elements.sent-2411-009: In analogy with the visible sector, which features many more particles than are necessary to explain the atoms and photons we interact with daily, it is reasonable to assume that DM is part of a larger set of particles, referred to as a dark sector.
elements.sent-2411-010: Realistic models should at least explain why DM is stable, how it achieves the observed abundance, and why it has not yet been detected.
elements.sent-2411-011: In this work, we present a DM model based on a dark sector with a new confining gauge force.
elements.sent-2411-012: DM stability is ensured by an unbroken flavor symmetry in the dark sector, which makes some of the lightest dark sector states, the dark pions, stable.
elements.sent-2411-013: The relic abundance of DM is determined by annihilation into unstable dark pions (termed transient dark pions) of the same mass, providing a simple realization of the impeded DM paradigm~\cite{Kopp:2016yji .}
elements.sent-2411-014: This mechanism introduces a velocity dependence to the annihilation rate, suppressing late-time annihilations and thereby sneakily circumventing stringent constraints from indirect detection.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, the model is viable for DM masses in the GeV range, which are typically excluded for thermal production mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-016: In fact after taking into account constraints from direct detection, the preferred regions of parameter space fall either between $1-10$~GeV or above 200~GeV, with transient dark pion lifetimes required to be macroscopic ($c\tau > 1$~mm) to avoid overly strong bounds from direct detection.
elements.sent-2411-017: Composite DM arising from pion-like states in confining dark sectors has been extensively studied (e.g.,~\cite{Essig:2009nc,Bhattacharya:2013kma,Cline:2013zca,Hochberg:2014kqa,Harigaya:2016rwr,Kopp:2016yji,Berlin:2018pwi,Beauchesne:2018myj,Beauchesne:2019ato,Bernreuther:2019pfb,Contino:2020god,Chu:2024rrv,Garcia-Cely:2024ivo ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: For instance, \cite{Beauchesne:2018myj examines a similar setup but focuses on the case of $n_f=3$ dark quark copies, such that several couplings have to be set to zero to ensure DM stability.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In contrast, we demonstrate that for $n_f \geq 4$, DM stability follows naturally from a dark flavor symmetry, which also protects the DM candidates from radiatively induced decays.
elements.sent-2411-020: Moreover, this symmetry enforces degenerate dark pion masses, which is crucial to evade stringent indirect detection constraints in the low-mass regime.
elements.sent-2411-021: Compared to other models, our framework has offers several attractive features.
elements.sent-2411-022: The relic abundance primarily depends on the parameters of the confining dark sector and is independent of the mass and couplings of the heavy mediator connecting the dark and visible sectors.
elements.sent-2411-023: Since DM is among the lightest states in the dark sector, it is copiously produced in dark showers, making it directly accessible in collider experiments seeking such signatures.
elements.sent-2411-024: We find that the viable parameter space of the model is probed by a combination of collider, direct detection and flavor experiments.
elements.sent-2411-025: In the $1-10$~GeV DM mass range, the model exhibits a rich collider phenomenology, as the dark showers contain a large number of both long lived and stable dark pions.
elements.sent-2411-026: Consequently, its signatures are a combination of emerging jets~\cite{Schwaller:2015gea,Mies:2020mzw,Linthorne:2021oiz and semi-visible jets~\cite{Cohen:2015toa,Cohen:2017pzm , which can be effectively targeted by merging these search strategies.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: We perform a recast of the semi-visible and emerging jets searches, as well as conventional multi-jet and missing energy searches, which cover the limits of vanishing and infinite lifetime, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-028: Since DM is now efficiently produced in the dark shower, the missing energy search is also sensitive for promptly decaying dark pions.
elements.sent-2411-029: Constraints are obtained in the DM-mediator mass plane to facilitate comparison with direct detection, and benchmarks for future experimental studies are proposed.
elements.sent-2411-030: This paper is organized as shown in the table of contents.
elements.sent-2411-032: The proposed model comprises a strongly interacting QCD-like dark sector that interacts with the Standard Model (SM) through a scalar bi-fundamental.
elements.sent-2411-033: Specifically, in addition to the SM, we consider a $SU(N_d)$ gauge sector and $n_f$ Dirac fermions $Q_\alpha,\, \alpha=1,\ldots,n_f$, transforming in the fundamental representation of $SU(N_d)$.

elements.sent-2411-303: Let us finish the section by speculating on the prospects of future searches.
elements.sent-2411-304: Clearly a future hadron collider operating at higher collision energies can push the collider limits to higher mediators masses, and possibly probe the $5-10$~TeV region.
elements.sent-2411-305: A multi-TeV lepton collider can pair produce the mediators since they are electrically charged, and should be able to push the limits close to half the collider energy.
elements.sent-2411-306: In addition, radiation of a single mediator from a quark, converting it into a dark quark, or the loop induced pair production of dark quarks could lead to interesting signatures that could be explored in the future.
elements.sent-2411-307: Dark matter direct detection experiments have reached the neutrino fog~\cite{XENON:2024ijk at recoil energies corresponding to $\sim 10$~GeV DM masses, and are getting close for higher masses.}
elements.sent-2411-308: In the future one can therefore expect more incremental improvements in the sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-309: For the best motivated region of DM masses in the $1-10$~GeV region, there is however still a lot of room for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-310: Searches for rare meson decays could probe DM masses below the B meson mass, but only in the case of down-type couplings, again highlighting the importance of a comprehensive search program.
elements.sent-2411-311: The origin of DM constitutes one of the most important problems in particle physics.
elements.sent-2411-312: However, in practice, we know very little about its nature, and very distinct paradigms are still compatible with existing experimental searches.
elements.sent-2411-313: The long-time leading DM candidate, a weakly interacting massive particle, is not at its best due to the absence of new physics at colliders and in DM direct detection experiments.
elements.sent-2411-314: A possible way out of this impasse is that (light) new physics associated with DM is long-lived, since most of the searches so far assumed prompt decays.
elements.sent-2411-315: This is even more important for light new physics, since the lower the energy the more the data there is available.
elements.sent-2411-316: In this work, we have presented a model, or class of models, featuring viable DM candidates in the GeV range coming along with long-lived particles and a very rich collider phenomenology.
elements.sent-2411-317: More specifically, we have presented a fairly minimal composite model of DM based on a QCD-like dark sector featuring a $SU(N_d)$ gauge symmetry, $n_f$ dark quarks, and a heavy t-channel scalar mediator.
elements.sent-2411-318: We have demonstrated that for $n_f\ge 4$, the dark flavor symmetry $\mathcal{G}\equiv SU(n_f-3)\times U(1)$ guarantees the stability of a subset of the dark pions, which are our DM candidates.
elements.sent-2411-319: Their interaction with the rest of the dark pions (that we dubbed transient dark pions) and their subsequent decay to SM quarks controls the DM relic abundance as well as the cross-section in direct and indirect detection experiments, which become independent of the mass and couplings of the heavy mediator.
elements.sent-2411-320: Moreover, along the lines of models of impeded DM, due to the degeneracy of the dark pions and their pNGB nature, the co-annihilation cross-section depends linearly on the velocity and is suppressed at low temperatures, alleviating both direct and indirect experimental bounds and opening up the GeV-mass window for this sneaky DM, typically excluded for thermal production mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-321: For the specific case of $n_f=4$ (where the dark sector is comprised of 9 transient and 6 stable dark pions), we have demonstrated that the sneaky DM can be as light as a few $\mathrm{GeV}$, without contradicting current bounds from direct and indirect DM detection.
elements.sent-2411-322: For such a window, the transient dark pions are long-lived with $c\tau\gtrsim 1\,\mathrm{mm}$, leading to spectacular signals at colliders such as emerging or semi-visible jets.
elements.sent-2411-323: In particular, we have derived constraints for the mediator mass in the case of dark matter masses $\mathcal{O (1)\,\mathrm{GeV}$ using several existing searches by ATLAS and CMS and estimated the reach of future experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-324: For fixed values of the dark meson masses and the transient dark pion decay constant $f_D$, we have set bounds on the mediator masses as a function of the transient dark pions lifetime combining searches for four jets, two jets + MET, semi-visible jets as well as searches for emerging jets or bounds from flavour violating meson decays, excluding $\mathcal{O (1)\,\mathrm{TeV}$ mediators for order one values of the portal coupling.}
elements.sent-2411-325: Compared with similar models that give rise to dark showers at colliders, sneaky dark matter predicts a dark shower composed of both stable and long lived dark pions, which suggests a search that combines both emerging jets and semi-visible jets strategies.
elements.sent-2411-326: The model is minimal in the sense that no additional particles or mechanisms are required to explain dark matter, and thus represents a key benchmark model for collider searches for composite dark matter.
elements.sent-2411-327: Finally the DM is produced copiously in the shower - in the case of a detection, this will therefore open the possibility for probing dark matter properties with precision studies of dark showers.
elements.sent-2411-328: Possible future directions include studying the prospect of detecting sneaky DM at future colliders and in fixed target/beam dump experiments, as well as more careful studies of the cosmic ray spectra produced by our model.. .

elements.sent-2411-338: We take $SU(n_f)$ generators with the following normalization \begin{equation}Tr\big(T^a T^b\big)=\frac{1}{2}\delta^{ab}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-339: We can define the tensors $f^{abc}$ and $d^{abc}$ \begin{equation}\big[T^a,T^b\big]=if^{abc}T^c,\quad d_{abc}=2Tr\Big(\big\{T^a,T^b\big\}T^c\Big)\end{equation} which are totally anti-symmetric and totally symmetric, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-341: This relationship can also be written as \begin{equation}Tr\Big[T^aT^b T^c T^d\Big] =\frac{1}{4n_f}\big(\delta^{ab}\delta^{cd}-\delta^{ac}\delta^{bd}+\delta^{ad}\delta^{bc}\big)+\frac{1}{8}\big(d^{abe}d^{cde}-d^{ace}d^{bde}+d^{ade}d^{bce}\big)\nonumber +\frac{i}{8}\big(d^{abe}f^{cde}+d^{ace}f^{bde}+d^{ade}f^{bce}\big).\end{equation}. elements.sent-2411-374: The selection criteria used for the recast of the non-resonant dijet, jets plus missing energy and semi-visible jets searches are given in table~\ref{table:selection .}
elements.sent-2411-375: We discuss them here in more detail.
elements.sent-2411-376: In the non-resonant dijet search events are selected if they have $N_{jets \geq 4$ with $p_T > 80$~GeV, $|\eta| < 2.5$, the scalar $p_T$ sum $H_T > 1050$~GeV or at least one jet with $p_T > 550$.}
elements.sent-2411-377: In addition to these selection criteria the four hardest jets are combined to two dijets based on the combination that minimizes $\Delta R = |\left(\Delta R_1-0.8\right)|+|\left(\Delta R_2-0.8\right)|$, where $\Delta R_i$ is the $\eta-\phi$ separation between two jets.
elements.sent-2411-378: Based on the minimizing combination signal events are also required to have $\Delta R_{1,2 <2$, $\Delta\eta = |\eta_1-\eta_2| < 1.1$ and an asymmetry $= \frac{|m_1-m_2| {m_1+m_2 < 0.1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-379: The small asymmetry requirement is based on the assumption that two close jets originate from the same resonance, so that the two dijets are close in mass.
elements.sent-2411-380: This is not the case here, however, because, if combined correctly, one jet in a dijet contains stable dark pions.
elements.sent-2411-381: Thus, this requirement reduces the efficiency of this search for our scenario drastically as can be seen by the purple lines in Fig.~\ref{fig:efficiency .}
elements.sent-2411-382: For large dark pion lifetimes the $H_T$ and $p_T$ requirements are no longer fulfilled and the efficiency decreases even further.
elements.sent-2411-383: The two jets plus MET search at CMS the requires at least two jets $N_{jet}\geq 2$ with $|\eta| < 2.4$ and $p_T > 30$~GeV.
elements.sent-2411-384: Additional requirements demand that the scalar $p_T$ sum $H_T > 300$~GeV for jets with $|\eta| < 2.4$ and the absolute value of the negative $p_T$ vector sum $H_T^{miss > 300$~GeV for jets with $|\eta|<5$.}
elements.sent-2411-385: To avoid mismeasurement events need to fulfill $H_T^{miss < H_T$.}
elements.sent-2411-386: Events with isolated electrons (muons) with $I < 0.1 (0.2)$, where $I$ is the scalar $p_T$ sum of hadrons and photons in a cone around the leptons over the letpon $p_T$ are excluded.
elements.sent-2411-387: Finally, an azimuthal angle between $\vec{H _T^{miss}$ and two highest $p_T$ jets $\Delta\phi(\vec{H _T^{miss ,j_{1,2 ) > 0.5$ and for lower $p_T$ jets $\Delta\phi(\vec{H _T^{miss ,j_{>2 ) > 0.3$ is required for signal events.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-388: Based on these selection criteria the search remains efficient even at small lifetimes, as stable dark pions produced in jets originating from dark quarks are enough to fulfill the CMS selection criteria.
elements.sent-2411-389: At around $10$~mm the efficiency increases, since more and more dark pions are stable on collider scales and the criteria on missing energy and $\Delta\Phi$ are fulfilled for more events.
elements.sent-2411-390: Finally, for the semi-visible search selection criteria on the number of jets, $N_{jet}\geq 2$ with $p_T > 30$~GeV and $|\eta|< 2.8$, the scalar $p_T$ sum $H_T > 600$~GeV, leading jet momentum $p_T > 250$~GeV and missing energy $H_T^{miss > 600$~GeV are used.}
elements.sent-2411-391: Moreover, no muons with $p_T > 7$~GeV in $|\eta|< 2.5$ and no electrons with $p_T > 7$~GeV in $|\eta|< 2.5$ and $1.37< |\eta| < 1.52$ are allowed for signal events.
elements.sent-2411-392: Events with more than one b-jet are rejected and at least one jet with $\Delta\phi(\vec{H _T^{miss ,j) = 2$ is required to pass the signal selection.}}
elements.sent-2411-393: From Fig.~\ref{fig:efficiency it can be seen that this search has the smallest efficiencies.}
elements.sent-2411-394: Because dark quarks are mainly produced via t-channel mediator exchange they lead to low momentum dark jets, so that the $H_T^{miss}$ requirement rules out most events.
elements.sent-2411-395: As expected the search becomes more efficient for larger dark pion lifetimes, since, similarly to the jets plus MET search, the MET requirements becomes more often fulfilled the more dark pions become stable on collider scales.
elements.sent-2411-396: An interesting behaviour can be seen in the case for couplings to down quarks: Dark pions with lower masses can be searched for more efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-397: This can be explained by the veto on b-jets: As soon as the b-channel is kinematically allowed most dark pions decay to b quarks leading to the decrease of the efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-398: The limits are given for six values of $R_{inv}$, which in our model increases as the lifetime of the transient dark pions increases.
elements.sent-2411-399: In practice we determine $R_{inv}$ as the fraction of produced dark pions that are either stable or decay outside of the hadronic calorimeter based on simulated events.
elements.sent-2411-400: We find $R_{inv}$ values ranging from $\sim0.3$ for prompt decays to $\sim0.95$, when transient dark pions are long-lived, and apply the appropriate limits.

elements.sent-2411-407: A striking consequence of the production of dark quarks is that they undergo showering and hadronization in the dark sector, leading to spectacular signatures such as emerging jets~\cite{Schwaller:2015gea or semi-visible jets~\cite{Cohen:2020afv , which are now searched for by the ATLAS and CMS experiments~\cite{CMS:2018bvr,CMS:2021dzg,ATLAS:2022aaw,CMS:2024gxp .}}}
